title: Healthy Affordable Housing Act
plain_summary: The Healthy Affordable Housing Act tells the Housing Secretary to start a new program that gives grants and loans to groups that want to build affordable housing. This housing must be built close to important places like health centers, grocery stores, and child care centers. The program will get 100 million dollars each year for five years to help make this happen. The government will also ask people who live in this housing questions to learn if living near these services helps them.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Healthy Affordable Housing Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Grant and Loan Program for Affordable Dwelling Units
sections.text:
The Secretary of Housing and Urban Development must create a program within one year of this law being passed. This program will give money through grants and loans to qualified groups. These groups can use the money to build, create, or fix up affordable housing in neighborhoods that do not have enough affordable homes.

To get money from this program, qualified groups must submit an application to the Secretary. The Secretary will decide when and how to submit these applications.

The Secretary can only give grants and loans for affordable housing projects in neighborhoods that have at least one of these nearby services: a Federally qualified health center (a government-approved community health clinic), a doctor who accepts Medicaid and provides basic medical care, a grocery store that accepts food stamps and WIC benefits, a licensed child care provider who cares for children they are not related to, a pharmacy, or public transportation like buses or trains.

The Secretary will give special preference to applications for housing projects that are within one mile of two or more of these services, or housing projects that will include some of these services in the same building.

The Secretary can set rules for choosing which applications to approve based on how many affordable housing units will be created, the neighborhood boundaries where the housing will be built, and the average income of people in that area.

Two years after people first move into any affordable housing built with this money, the Secretary must survey the residents every two years for ten years. The survey will ask residents about any benefits they see from living near the required services. The Secretary can also survey people who do not live in these housing units to compare their answers.

The Secretary must write a report about the survey results within one year of completing each survey. These reports go to specific committees in both the House of Representatives and the Senate. Each report must check whether any of the nearby required services have closed or moved since the last report.

This law does not stop the Secretary from giving money to someone who has also applied for other housing funding from the government. It also does not stop these affordable housing units from receiving rental assistance under Section 8 housing programs.

Congress approves spending one hundred million dollars each year from two thousand twenty five to two thousand twenty nine to run this program.

This section defines important terms used in the law. An eligible entity means a local government including counties, a tribe or tribal housing organization, someone who owns or develops affordable housing, a public housing agency, an organization whose mission involves affordable housing work, or any combination of these groups. A Federally qualified health center has the same meaning as defined in Social Security law. A qualifying affordable dwelling unit means a housing unit that meets the affordable housing rules and income requirements set in the Cranston-Gonzalez National Affordable Housing Act. Secretary means the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development.
